questions.question: What is the main function of I/O devices in a computer system?

options.A: To provide an interface between the computer and its environment.
options.C: To increase the processing speed of the CPU.

questions.explanation: I/O devices are the interface between the computer and its environment; they handle communication between the machine and external entities (keyboard, mouse, display, etc.).

questions.question: In POSIX system calls, what is typically the semantic behind the return values?
questions.explanation: POSIX convention: 0 means success, any non-zero value indicates an error. This is the opposite of the 'boolean success' convention used in some other languages.

questions.question: What is a Device Driver?

options.A: It is a software that allows the interaction with a hardware component.
options.B: It is a hardware component of an external device that receives requests from the processor.
options.C: It is a class of software specifically tailored to configure the processor timer.
options.D: It is a software component that enables direct interaction with hardware without the involvement of the operating system.

questions.explanation: A Device Driver (DD) is software that enables higher-level code (OS or applications) to interact with a hardware component without knowing its low-level details.

questions.question: What is the behavior of the driver_receive system call in Minix?

options.A: It blocks until a software interrupt is received, allowing for context switches.
options.B: It errors if there are no notifications pending for the calling process.
options.C: It polls the PIC for interruptions of relevant peripherals.
options.D: It blocks until a service request message is received, allowing for context switches.

questions.explanation: driver_receive() is a blocking call: the driver process suspends until either an interrupt notification or a service-request message arrives, freeing the CPU for other tasks in the meantime.

questions.question: For the function timer_set_frequency, a frequency divisor d was required. Which formula correctly expresses its calculation? In this context, freq is the desired frequency, TIMER_FREQ is the clock frequency for the timer in the PC, and USHRT_MAX is the maximum value of an unsigned short.
questions.explanation: The divisor d = TIMER_FREQ / freq, where TIMER_FREQ = 1 193 181 Hz. Loading d into the i8254 counter makes the timer interrupt at the desired frequency.

questions.question: A Device Driver wants to subscribe to timer interrupts to be notified by the kernel via a payload with bit 7 set. What should be the value of the hook_id argument passed to the sys_irqsetpolicy function?
questions.explanation: The hook_id input value is the bit number. If hook_id=7, the kernel sets bit 7 (BIT(7)) of msg.m_notify.interrupts when the interrupt fires. So hook_id=7 produces a notification with bit 7 set.

questions.question: During class you have studied and used the sys_inb(int port, uint8_t *value) function. What does it do?

options.A: Reads a word value from the port register, and returns 0 in case of success.
options.B: Writes a word value to the port register, and returns 0 in case of success.
options.C: Reads a word port from the value register, and returns 0 in case of success.
options.D: Writes a word port to the value register, and returns 0 in case of success.

questions.explanation: sys_inb(int port, uint32_t *val) reads a byte from the specified I/O port and returns 0 on success. It wraps the IN x86 assembly instruction and requires kernel privilege.

questions.question: Which would be a valid makecode and breakcode combination?
questions.explanation: The breakcode is the makecode with bit 7 set. 0x1E = 0001 1110; setting bit 7 gives 0x9E = 1001 1110. The other options have the make/break relationship reversed.

questions.question: In the KBC, why are there scancodes with two bytes?

options.B: We could not fit the entire set of scan codes in one byte.
options.C: It allows the scan codes to map to different keyboard language mappings.

questions.explanation: The original PC had a limited scancode set. Extended keys (arrow keys, numpad, etc.) were added later for backward compatibility using the escape byte 0xE0 followed by a second byte.

questions.question: To read keyboard scancodes you can choose between polling and using interrupts. Which one do you expect to perform the least amount of sys_inb() kernel calls?

options.C: The number of calls is random.
options.D: They always perform the same number of calls.

questions.explanation: With interrupts, sys_inb() is called only when the KBC has data ready. With polling, sys_inb() is called continuously on the status register even when no key is pressed, resulting in far more kernel calls.

questions.question: Choose the right statement.

options.A: The interrupt mechanism allows for more CPU useful time.
options.B: The polling mechanism allows for more CPU useful time.
options.C: The polling mechanism is always more efficient than the interrupt mechanism.
options.D: The interrupt mechanism implies active await by the CPU.

questions.explanation: Interrupts allow the CPU to execute other code while waiting for an I/O event. Polling burns CPU cycles checking for events. So interrupts free the CPU for useful work, unlike active polling (busy-waiting).

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Status Register in the KBC?

options.A: To store the last typed character inside KBC.
options.B: To display keyboard input on the screen.
options.C: To store the state of the KBC.

questions.explanation: The KBC Status Register (port 0x64) reflects the current state of the controller: whether the output buffer is full (OBF), input buffer is full (IBF), parity errors, and other flags.

questions.question: What does the IRQ_REENABLE flag do in the context of interrupt notification subscriptions in Minix?

options.A: It makes it so one can later remove the respective subscription.
options.B: It raises the priority of the installed interrupt handler, preventing the kernel from handling it first.
options.C: It allows the bundled Device Driver in Minix to handle interrupts from the respective IRQ line once the subscription is removed.
options.D: It makes it so the kernel unmasks interrupts on the IRQ line associated with the specified hook_id automatically.

questions.explanation: When IRQ_REENABLE is set in sys_irqsetpolicy(), the kernel's Generic Interrupt Handler automatically unmasks the IRQ line in the PIC after notifying the driver, so subsequent interrupts are not lost.

questions.question: What is the function of the control register at port 0x43 in the i8254?

options.B: Controls data transfer between CPU and memory.
options.D: Programs the timers and selects counting modes.

questions.explanation: Port 0x43 is the i8254 Control Register. Writing a control word to it configures one of the three timers (counter select, read/write mode, counting mode). Then the initial count is written to the timer's own port.

questions.question: What does the Read-Back command in the i8254 allow?

options.A: Increasing the processing speed of the timer.
options.C: Reading the current counter value and programmed configuration.
options.D: Resetting the timer to its default settings.

questions.explanation: The Read-Back command (written to 0x43) allows reading the current count value and/or the programmed configuration (status) of one or more timers without disturbing their operation.

questions.question: The PIC (Priority Interrupt Controller) is responsible for:

options.A: Warning the CPU of a new interrupt.
options.B: Warning the I/O device of interrupts by the correct order.
options.C: Executing the respective interrupt handler for each interrupt.
options.D: Warning the CPU that the interrupt handler stopped executing.

questions.explanation: The PIC collects interrupt requests from multiple devices, applies a priority scheme, and raises the CPU's INT line to signal that a device needs attention. The CPU then acknowledges and processes the interrupt.

questions.question: In Lab 2, how was time measured and a message printed every second?

options.A: Using the Read-Back command every second.
options.B: By subscribing the timer interrupts and using the 60Hz Minix setting.
options.C: By setting the timer with a 120Hz frequency.
options.D: By subscribing the timer interrupts and using the 120Hz Minix setting.

questions.explanation: Minix programs Timer 0 at 60 Hz by default. Subscribing to timer interrupts and counting 60 ticks gives exactly one second. Using 120 Hz would be incorrect because Minix's default is 60 Hz.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the i8254 timer / counter on a PC?

options.B: To measure time intervals and generate clock signals.

questions.explanation: The i8254 is a programmable timer/counter that measures time precisely and can generate periodic interrupts and square waves, independently of the processor speed.

questions.question: What will be the output of (12 & 10)?
questions.explanation: 12 = 1100, 10 = 1010. Bitwise AND: 1100 & 1010 = 1000 = 8. Only the bit that is 1 in BOTH numbers survives the AND.

questions.question: When a key is pressed, how is the scancode sent to the PC?

options.A: The keyboard controller generates a scancode and sends it to the PC via a buffer.
options.C: The CPU manually checks each key for its state.
options.D: The keyboard sends an electrical pulse to the CPU.

questions.explanation: When a key is pressed, the controller at the keyboard generates a scancode (makecode) and places it in a serial buffer for transmission to the PC's KBC (i8042), which then signals the CPU via IRQ 1.
